abstract: This paper introduces the Polynomial Mixer (PoM), a novel token mixing mechanism with linear complexity that serves as a drop-in replacement for self-attention. PoM aggregates input tokens into a compact representation through a learned polynomial function, from which each token retrieves contextual information. We prove that PoM satisfies the contextual mapping property, ensuring that transformers equipped with PoM remain universal sequence-to-sequence approximators. We replace standard self-attention with PoM across five diverse domains: text generation, handwritten text recognition, image generation, 3D modeling, and Earth observation. PoM matches the performance of attention-based models while drastically reducing computational cost when working with long sequences. The code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin. Attention is all you need. In NeurIPS, 2017.
citations.justification: This is the foundational paper that introduced the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism and the Transformer architecture. The main paper's proposed Polynomial Mixer (PoM) is explicitly designed as a direct, linear-time replacement for MHA, making this the primary work that PoM aims to improve upon in terms of efficiency.
citations.title: Attention is all you need

citations.fullCitation: Chulhee Yun, Srinadh Bhojanapalli, Ankit Singh Rawat, Sashank Reddi, and Sanjiv Kumar. Are transformers universal approximators of sequence-to-sequence functions? In ICLR, 2020.
citations.justification: This paper provides the theoretical framework for proving that Transformers are universal approximators, which hinges on the 'contextual mapping property' of attention. The PoM paper leverages this exact framework to prove that their proposed mixer also satisfies this property, thereby theoretically validating it as a powerful replacement for attention without sacrificing expressivity.
citations.title: Are transformers universal approximators of sequence-to-sequence functions?

citations.fullCitation: Tri Dao, Dan Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, and Christopher Ré. FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with io-awareness. In NeurIPS, 2022.
citations.justification: A key claim of the main paper is the superior efficiency of PoM. FlashAttention is a highly optimized implementation of the standard attention mechanism that serves as a critical, state-of-the-art baseline. The paper repeatedly benchmarks PoM's performance against FlashAttention to demonstrate its practical speed advantages for long sequences.
citations.title: FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with io-awareness

citations.fullCitation: Albert Gu, Karan Goel, and Christopher Re. Efficiently modeling long sequences with structured state spaces. In ICLR, 2021.
citations.justification: This paper is a foundational work on State Space Models (SSMs), which are presented as a major class of alternatives to attention for efficient sequence modeling. The PoM paper positions its work within this broader context, contrasting its approach with SSMs, making this citation important for understanding the landscape of competing architectures.
citations.title: Efficiently modeling long sequences with structured state spaces

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper titled "PoM: A Linear-Time Replacement for Attention with the Polynomial Mixer."

---

## Research Paper Analysis: PoM: A Linear-Time Replacement for Attention with the Polynomial Mixer

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The paper is authored by David Picard, Nicolas Dufour, Lucas Degeorge, Arijit Ghosh, Davide Allegro, Tom Ravaud, Yohann Perron, Corentin Sautier, Zeynep Sonat Baltaci, Fei Meng, Syrine Kalleli, Marta López-Rauhut, Thibaut Loiseau, Ségolène Albouy, Raphael Baena, Elliot Vincent, and Loic Landrieu.

Their affiliations are primarily with:
*   **LIGM, CNRS, Univ Gustave Eiffel, ENPC, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, France** (most authors)
*   **LIX, École Polytechnique, CNRS, IP Paris, France** (Nicolas Dufour, Lucas Degeorge)
*   **Department of Information Engineering, Università degli Studi di Padova, Italy** (Davide Allegro)
*   **AMIAD, Pole recherche, France** (Lucas Degeorge)
*   **EFEO, France** (Yohann Perron)
*   **Valeo.ai, France** (Corentin Sautier)
*   **LASTIG, Univ Gustave Eiffel, IGN, Géodata Paris, Paris, France** (Elliot Vincent)

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The Transformer architecture, particularly its Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism, has been foundational to advances in various artificial intelligence domains, including large language models, generative models for images and videos, computer vision, and speech processing. However, MHA exhibits a quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to the input sequence length. This quadratic scaling limits the context size that models can process efficiently, especially for applications involving long sequences such as high-resolution images, videos, or extensive text documents.

The broader research landscape has seen numerous efforts to address this limitation. These efforts generally fall into two categories:
1.  **Faster Attention Implementations and Approximations:** This includes methods like Reformer, which uses approximate neighbors to reduce attention matrix size, or Linformer, which applies low-rank projections. More recent advancements, such as FlashAttention and KV-cache optimization, focus on improving the efficiency of exact attention computation through optimized hardware-aware kernels. While these methods improve efficiency, some, like Linformer, introduce constraints such as fixed sequence length, or, in the case of FlashAttention, still exhibit quadratic scaling for very long sequences, particularly when sequence length grows quadratically with resolution (e.g., in visual content).
2.  **Alternative Architectures:** This category seeks to replace or bypass self-attention entirely. Examples include MLP-Mixer and ResMLP, which replace MHA with simple projections, but also often impose fixed sequence lengths. State-Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba have gained traction, especially in language modeling, due to their recurrent nature allowing for constant complexity in autoregressive generation. However, SSMs often impose a causal ordering, which may not be naturally suited for all modalities, such as 2D spatial data in images, requiring arbitrary scan orders.

This paper introduces the Polynomial Mixer (PoM) as a novel contribution to this landscape. PoM aims to provide a token mixing mechanism with linear complexity that serves as a direct, drop-in replacement for self-attention. The work questions the necessity of quadratic complexity to achieve MHA-comparable performance while attempting to overcome the limitations of existing linear-time alternatives (e.g., fixed sequence length, imposed causality) and retain the expressivity of Transformers.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a novel token mixing mechanism, the Polynomial Mixer (PoM), that addresses the computational and memory scalability limitations of Multi-Head Attention (MHA) in Transformer architectures. The key objectives and underlying motivations can be itemized as follows:

1.  **Achieve Linear Computational Complexity:** The fundamental motivation is to overcome the quadratic complexity of MHA with respect to input sequence length. This quadratic scaling is identified as a significant bottleneck, preventing the application of Transformers to tasks requiring processing of very long sequences, such as high-resolution images, videos, or extended text contexts, without substantial computational cost or memory footprint. PoM is designed to operate with linear complexity, enabling efficient processing of longer sequences.

2.  **Maintain Performance Parity with MHA:** A critical objective is for PoM to serve as a drop-in replacement for self-attention while achieving comparable or superior performance across a diverse range of tasks and modalities. Many prior attempts to reduce attention's complexity have involved trade-offs in performance or generality. This work seeks to demonstrate that linear complexity does not inherently necessitate a reduction in model capability.

3.  **Preserve Generality and Expressivity:** The paper aims to ensure that PoM-equipped Transformers retain the desirable properties of standard Transformers. Specifically, PoM is motivated by the need to satisfy the "contextual mapping property," which is crucial for Transformers to function as universal sequence-to-sequence approximators. This property allows the model to uniquely summarize context while preserving the identity of individual tokens, a feature not commonly found in other linear-time alternatives like MLPs, CNNs, or many State Space Models. The authors explicitly set out to prove this property for PoM.

4.  **Avoid Constraints of Existing Linear-Time Models:** The research is motivated by the limitations of current sub-quadratic or linear-time alternatives. For instance, some methods require fixed sequence lengths (e.g., Linformer, MLP-Mixer), which restricts their applicability. Others, like State Space Models, impose causal ordering, which is suitable for language but less natural for non-causal modalities like images. PoM seeks to offer a more general solution that is adaptable to both causal and non-causal contexts without arbitrary constraints.

5.  **Demonstrate Versatility Across Diverse Domains:** A key objective is to empirically validate PoM's effectiveness across a broad spectrum of real-world applications and data modalities. This includes text generation (Natural Language Processing), image generation, optical character recognition (OCR), 3D point cloud segmentation, and Earth observation analysis. This multi-domain evaluation aims to establish PoM as a general-purpose building block, rather than a specialized solution for a single task.

In summary, the motivation behind PoM is to develop a scalable, efficient, and versatile token mixing mechanism that can replace MHA, thereby extending the applicability of Transformer-like architectures to contexts with significantly longer sequence lengths without compromising model performance or theoretical expressivity.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The Polynomial Mixer (PoM) is introduced as a token mixing mechanism designed to replace Multi-Head Attention (MHA) while operating with linear complexity. The methodology involves defining the PoM module, integrating it into a Transformer-like block, adapting it for causal sequences, and providing theoretical guarantees for its expressivity.

**4.1. Polynomial Mixer (PoM) Module**

The core of PoM lies in aggregating input tokens into a compact, shared representation and then allowing each token to query this representation.

*   **Input Representation:** The input is a sequence of *n* tokens, each with dimension *d*, represented as a tensor $X \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$.
*   **Shared State Representation (H(X)):** Unlike MHA's pairwise interactions, PoM summarizes the entire sequence into a single shared state $H(X) \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1}$, where *D* is an internal feature dimension. This summary is computed using a learned polynomial function:
    $$ H(X) = \left[ \sum_{p=1}^{k} \alpha_p \odot (h(W_h X))^p \right] \mathbf{1} $$
    Here, $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$ is a learnable matrix that projects input tokens to the internal dimension *D*. $h$ is an activation function. The operation $(h(W_h X))^p$ denotes element-wise exponentiation. $\alpha_p \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times k}$ are learnable polynomial coefficients. The $\odot$ symbol denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product with broadcasting, and $\mathbf{1}$ is a vector of ones used to sum across the sequence dimension *n*, collapsing the *n* tokens into a single vector. This aggregation process is linear in *n*.
*   **Token Query and Output Projection:** Each token then retrieves contextual information from $H(X)$. This is done by computing gating coefficients $S(X) = \sigma(W_s X) \in [0, 1]^{D \times n}$, where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function and $W_s \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$ is another learnable matrix. These gating coefficients element-wise multiply the broadcasted shared state $H(X)$ (which is replicated *n* times to match the sequence dimension). Finally, the resulting information is projected back to the original dimension *d* by $W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times D}$:
    $$ \text{PoM}(X) = W_o [\sigma(W_s X) \odot H(X)] $$
    All these operations are designed to be linear in the sequence length *n*.

**4.2. PolyMorpher Block**

To integrate PoM into a Transformer-like architecture, the authors define a PolyMorpher block *P* that follows the Transformer's residual structure:
$$ P(X) = X + \text{PoM}(X) + \text{FF}(X + \text{PoM}(X)) $$
where $\text{FF}(X)$ is a standard two-layer feed-forward network. This design positions PoM as a direct replacement for MHA blocks within a Transformer, with its parametrization defined by the polynomial degree *k* and the internal dimension *D*.

**4.3. PolyMorpher for Causal Sequences**

PoM can be adapted to handle causal dependencies. For a sequence with a binary mask $M \in \{0, 1\}^{n \times n}$ where $M_{s,t}=1$ indicates token *s* can use information from token *t*:
$$ H(X) = \left[ \sum_{p=1}^{k} \alpha_p \odot (h(W_h X))^p \right] M^\top $$
Here, $H(X) \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times n}$, meaning each token has its own causal state summary.

*   **Causal Sequence:** For strictly causal sequences (lower triangular mask), the state representation $H(X)_t$ at time step *t* can be computed iteratively:
    $$ H(X)_t = H(X)_{t-1} + \left[ \sum_{p=1}^{k} \alpha_p \odot (h(W_h X))^p \right]_t $$
    This iterative computation enables O(1) inference complexity in autoregressive settings, similar to recurrent networks.
*   **Block Causal Sequence:** PoM also supports block-causal sequences, common in spatio-temporal data (e.g., videos). For a block size *K*, the state representation can be computed by summing within the current block and incorporating the state from the previous block, allowing for block-wise processing during inference.

**4.4. Theoretical Analysis**

The paper provides theoretical insights into PoM's properties:

*   **Permutation Equivariance:** PoM is proven to be permutation equivariant (Proposition 3.1). This means that permuting the input sequence results in a corresponding permutation of the output, a key property shared with MHA that makes Transformers suitable for permutation-invariant tasks (when positional encodings are not used).
*   **Universal Approximation Theorem:** The authors prove that a PolyMorpher with learned positional encoding is a universal sequence-to-sequence approximator (Theorem 3.2). This establishes that PoM-based Transformers can approximate any continuous sequence-to-sequence function, similar to MHA-based Transformers.
*   **Contextual Mapping Property:** A crucial element for universal approximation is the contextual mapping property (Lemma 3.3), which PoM satisfies for a sufficiently high polynomial degree *k*. This property ensures that PoM can map every token to a unique value that depends on the entire sequence while preserving the identity of the current token, allowing subsequent feed-forward networks to perform the desired mapping.

**4.5. Complexity and Efficiency Analysis**

The computational cost of PoM is analyzed and compared to MHA.
*   **PoM Cost:** Approximately $3ndD + (k+1)nD$ multiplications, which is linear in *n*.
*   **MHA Cost:** Approximately $4nd^2 + 2dn^2$ multiplications, which is quadratic in *n*.

The analysis suggests PoM becomes faster than MHA when $n \ge \frac{D(3d + k + 1) - 4d^2}{2d}$. For typical parameters ($D=2d$, $k=2$), PoM offers advantages for sequence lengths greater than $d+3$, often around 1k tokens. While optimized attention kernels (like FlashAttention) can be competitive for shorter sequences, PoM's linear scaling leads to significant efficiency gains for long sequences. The PoM implementation used is in high-level PyTorch, suggesting potential for further speedups with low-level optimization.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The Polynomial Mixer (PoM) was evaluated across five diverse domains, demonstrating its performance and efficiency compared to standard self-attention (MHA) and other linear-time alternatives.

**5.1. Natural Language Processing (NLP)**
*   **Task:** 125M GPT-2 models trained on 15B tokens from FineWeb, evaluated on validation loss and benchmarks (ARC-E, HellaSwag, Winogrande, MMLU).
*   **Architectures:**
    *   **GPPoM2:** All self-attention blocks replaced by Polymorphers.
    *   **GPPoM2 Hyb.:** Pairs of self-attention blocks replaced by one Polymorpher and one local-attention block (causal context limited to 128 tokens).
*   **Results (Table 1a, 1b):**
    *   GPPoM2 achieved comparable, though slightly lower, performance than GPT2-S (MHA) on benchmarks.
    *   GPPoM2 Hybrid matched or slightly surpassed GPT2-S performance across most benchmarks, including OpenAI's checkpoint.
    *   Both GPPoM variants demonstrated substantial efficiency gains. PoM-based models were notably faster than GPT2-S MHA (e.g., 447.5 k-tokens/s vs. 52.4 k-tokens/s for 32k sequence length) and also faster than Mamba at longer sequence lengths (e.g., 447.5 vs. 56.7 k-tokens/s for 32k sequence length).
    *   The polynomial degree *k* had minimal impact on speed or performance within the tested range.

**5.2. Optical Character Recognition (OCR)**
*   **Task:** Handwritten text recognition using HTR-VT model on the LAM dataset (single-line and multi-line prediction).
*   **Architectures:**
    *   **HTR-VPoM:** All attention layers replaced by PoM layers.
    *   **HTR-VPoM Hybrid:** Alternating attention layers and PoM layers (full attention, no causal mask).
*   **Results (Table 2):**
    *   **Single-line prediction (128 tokens):** PoM-based architectures were on-par with HTR-VT (MHA) in Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER). Throughput was similar due to short sequence length.
    *   **Multi-line prediction (up to 2k tokens):** HTR-VPoM Hybrid was on par with HTR-VT in CER/WER, while increasing throughput by approximately 6%. HTR-VPoM (pure PoM) had a minor degradation in CER (0.4 worse) but achieved a significant 12% speedup compared to FlashAttention. PoM models exhibited higher throughput in the multi-line setup than single-line, indicating superior scalability.

**5.3. Earth Observation Analysis**
*   **Task:** Crop type mapping from satellite multi-spectral image time series on the PASTIS dataset.
*   **Architecture:** TSViPoM (PoM replaces all Transformer blocks in TSViT).
*   **Results (Table 3):**
    *   TSViPoM achieved comparable overall accuracy (OA) and mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) to the TSViT (MHA) baseline.
    *   TSViPoM demonstrated a substantial speedup, with over 300% faster inference throughput compared to TSViT. This highlights PoM's efficiency for spatio-temporal modeling with long temporal sequences.

**5.4. 3D Point Cloud Segmentation**
*   **Task:** Semantic segmentation on ScanNet (indoor scenes) and SemanticKITTI (autonomous driving) using PointTransformerV3 (PTv3).
*   **Architectures:**
    *   **PPoMv3:** All MHA layers replaced by PoM layers.
    *   **PPoMv3-Hybrid:** Alternating MHA and PoM layers (with context window of 4096 points in highest-resolution stages).
*   **Results (Table 4):**
    *   PPoMv3 Hybrid matched or slightly surpassed PTv3 (MHA) performance on both datasets, with a 2% reduction in computation time.
    *   Pure PPoMv3 models were up to 10% faster than FlashAttention, with a minor accuracy drop.
    *   A practical advantage noted was PoM's ability to process entire point clouds without chunking due to linear complexity, relevant for large-scale industrial applications.

**5.5. Class-Conditional Image Generation**
*   **Task:** Image generation on ImageNet (256x256 resolution) using the SiT architecture (a flow/diffusion-based Transformer).
*   **Architectures:** SiPoM-L/2 and SiPoM-XL/2 (PoM replaces all Attention blocks in SiT-L/2 and SiT-XL/2).
*   **Results (Table 5, Figure 4, 5):**
    *   SiPoM variants achieved comparable visual fidelity to original attention-based SiT models, as measured by FID (Fréchet Inception Distance). For example, SiPoM-XL/2 matched SiT-XL/2 with 17.2 FID.
    *   SiPoM models demonstrated higher throughput. SiPoM-XL/2 was approximately 2 times faster than SiT-XL/2 (8146 img/s vs 4041 img/s for batch size 1024).
    *   The efficiency gain for SiPoM was more pronounced at higher resolutions (e.g., 1024x1024 pixels per side), where the number of tokens grows quadratically, further accentuating the difference between linear PoM and quadratic attention costs.
    *   Qualitative results (Figure 5) showed that SiPoM-XL/2 could produce images of similar visual quality to its attention counterpart.

**Overall Findings:**
*   PoM, particularly in its hybrid form with some local attention, consistently achieved performance on par with or slightly better than MHA-based Transformers across five diverse domains.
*   PoM exhibited substantial speedups and superior scalability, especially for long sequences and high-resolution data, due to its linear computational complexity.
*   The linear scaling of PoM makes it more efficient for tasks where sequence length is a significant factor, offering throughput advantages over even highly optimized MHA implementations like FlashAttention in such regimes.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The introduction of the Polynomial Mixer (PoM) carries several points of significance and potential impact on the field of deep learning:

1.  **Addressing the Scalability Bottleneck of Transformers:** The most immediate significance of PoM is its ability to provide a linear-time token mixing mechanism that can directly replace the quadratically scaling self-attention. This addresses a critical limitation of the Transformer architecture, which has constrained its application to tasks involving very long sequences (e.g., high-resolution images, extensive documents, lengthy video clips). By offering linear complexity, PoM enables models to process longer contexts more efficiently in terms of both computation and memory.

2.  **Maintaining Performance with Enhanced Efficiency:** The empirical results across five diverse domains demonstrate that PoM, especially in hybrid configurations with some local attention, can match or even slightly surpass the performance of full-attention models. This indicates that the reduction in computational complexity does not necessarily entail a trade-off in model accuracy or representational capacity, which has been a challenge for many prior efficient attention alternatives. This finding is significant because it suggests that quadratic complexity may not be inherently necessary for high performance in sequence-to-sequence tasks.

3.  **Theoretical Guarantees for Expressivity:** The proof that PoM satisfies the contextual mapping property and that Polymorpher blocks retain the universal approximation capabilities of Transformers provides theoretical validation. This ensures that models incorporating PoM can still learn complex sequence-to-sequence mappings, differentiating PoM from other simplified architectures (like basic MLPs or CNNs) that lack these properties. This theoretical foundation lends confidence to PoM's potential as a general-purpose building block.

4.  **Versatility Across Modalities:** The successful application of PoM across Natural Language Processing, Optical Character Recognition, Earth Observation, 3D Point Cloud Segmentation, and Image Generation highlights its versatility. This suggests that PoM is not a niche solution but a general-purpose component adaptable to various data types and task requirements, including those with non-causal dependencies where State Space Models might be less inherently suited.

5.  **Enabling New Applications and Research Directions:**
    *   **High-Definition Media Processing:** For tasks like high-definition video generation or processing very large images, where token counts grow rapidly, PoM's linear scaling could be transformative, enabling models to operate on entire inputs without aggressive downsampling or chunking, preserving fine-grained details.
    *   **Long-Context Multimodal Models:** In the emerging field of multimodal large language models that integrate various long-sequence inputs (text, image, audio, video), PoM's efficiency could facilitate processing significantly richer and longer contexts, leading to more capable and versatile AI systems.
    *   **Efficient Autoregressive Inference:** The O(1) inference complexity under causal masking for PoM makes it particularly appealing for autoregressive generation tasks (e.g., text generation, sequential data forecasting), offering a more efficient alternative to standard Transformers in this critical aspect.

6.  **Foundation for Future Optimization:** The fact that the current PoM implementation is primarily in high-level PyTorch, yet still shows significant speedups over highly optimized FlashAttention in many long-sequence scenarios, suggests substantial potential for further gains through dedicated low-level hardware-aware kernel optimizations. This positions PoM as a promising direction for developing the next generation of efficient, general-purpose sequence models.

In conclusion, PoM represents a step towards developing more scalable and efficient deep learning architectures for sequence processing. Its ability to combine linear complexity with the expressivity and performance typically associated with quadratic attention mechanisms opens new avenues for tackling complex, data-intensive AI problems that were previously limited by computational constraints.
overview:
The Transformer architecture has become the standard for a wide array of artificial intelligence tasks, from large language models to high-resolution image generation. At its core is the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) mechanism, which allows models to weigh the importance of different parts of an input sequence relative to each other. However, MHA has a significant limitation: its computational and memory requirements grow quadratically with the length of the input sequence. This means that doubling the input length quadruples the resources required, making it difficult to process extremely long documents, high-resolution videos, or large 3D environments.

Research has increasingly focused on finding "sub-quadratic" alternatives that can approximate or replace attention without this scaling penalty. Existing approaches include State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba, which offer constant-time inference but often struggle with non-sequential data like images, and various low-rank or local attention approximations that can sometimes sacrifice model expressivity. The Polynomial Mixer (PoM) is a token mixing mechanism designed to replace MHA with linear computational complexity while maintaining the high performance and theoretical generality associated with standard Transformers.

## The Architecture of the Polynomial Mixer (PoM)

The primary goal of PoM is to allow each token in a sequence to access global context without performing the pairwise comparisons that lead to quadratic complexity. In a standard attention mechanism, every token queries every other token. In contrast, PoM summarizes the entire sequence into a single, compact "shared state" and then allows each individual token to query this state.

Suppose we have an input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$, where $n$ is the number of tokens and $d$ is the feature dimension of each token. The PoM operation involves two main steps: sequence aggregation and token-wise gating.

First, the model computes a shared state representation $H(X) \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1}$, where $D$ is an internal hidden dimension. This state is calculated using a learned polynomial function that aggregates information across all $n$ tokens:

$$
H(X) = \left[ \sum_{p=1}^{k} \alpha_p \odot (h(W_h X))^p \right] \mathbf{1} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1}
$$

In this equation, $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$ is a learnable weight matrix that projects tokens into the hidden space. The function $h$ is an activation function, and $(.)^p$ denotes an element-wise power operation. The learnable polynomial coefficients $\alpha_p \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1} \text{ for } p \in \{1, \dots, k\}$ allow the model to weight different orders of the polynomial up to a degree $k$. The final multiplication by $\mathbf{1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ (a vector of ones) performs a summation across the sequence dimension, effectively compressing $n$ tokens into a single vector.

Second, the model uses this global summary to update each token. It computes a gating mask $S(X) = \sigma(W_s X) \in [0, 1]^{D \times n}$ using a sigmoid function $\sigma(.)$ and another weight matrix $W_s \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$. This mask determines which parts of the shared global state are relevant to each specific token. The final output is then projected back to the original dimension $d$ using $W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times D}$:

$$
\text{PoM}(X) = W_o [\sigma(W_s X) \odot H(X)] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}
$$

Because both the aggregation and the gating steps involve operations that scale linearly with the number of tokens $n$, PoM achieves $O(n)$ complexity, making it significantly more efficient for long sequences than the $O(n^2)$ complexity of standard attention.

## Theoretical Expressivity and the PolyMorpher Block

A critical question for any attention replacement is whether it can still perform the complex reasoning required for modern AI. Theoretical analysis suggests that PoM-based models, referred to as PolyMorphers, retain several important properties of standard Transformers.

The PolyMorpher block is structured similarly to a Transformer block, using residual connections and feed-forward networks (FFN):

$$
P(X) = X + \text{PoM}(X) + \text{FF}(X + \text{PoM}(X))
$$

The authors demonstrate that PoM is permutation equivariant, meaning that if the order of the input tokens is shuffled, the output tokens will be shuffled in exactly the same way (provided no positional encodings are used). This property is a hallmark of self-attention and is vital for tasks where the spatial or temporal relationship between items must be learned rather than hard-coded.

More importantly, the research proves that PoM satisfies the "contextual mapping property." This property ensures that the mixer can map every token to a unique value that depends on the entire context of the sequence while still preserving the unique identity of the original token. Based on this, the PolyMorpher block is shown to be a universal sequence-to-sequence approximator. This means that, in theory, a network of PolyMorpher blocks can approximate any continuous function that maps one sequence to another, placing it on the same theoretical footing as the standard Transformer.

## Handling Causal Dependencies and Real-Time Inference

In natural language processing, models typically operate in a "causal" mode, where each token can only see tokens that came before it. While the basic PoM aggregates the entire sequence, it can be easily adapted for causal tasks.

In a causal setting, the shared state $H(X)$ is no longer a single vector for the whole sequence but becomes a sequence of states where each state $H(X)_t$ only includes information from tokens up to time $t$. This allows for very efficient autoregressive generation (e.g., predicting the next word in a sentence). The state can be updated iteratively:

$$
H(X)_t = H(X)_{t-1} + \sum_{p=1}^{k} \alpha_p \odot (h(W_h x_t))^p \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times 1}
$$

where $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 1}$ is the token at the current time step. This recurrence means that during inference, the cost of generating a new token is $O(1)$—it does not depend on how many tokens have already been generated. This provides a massive advantage over standard Transformers, where the "KV cache" grows with sequence length and eventually slows down generation or exceeds memory limits.

## Efficiency Benchmarks and Computational Complexity

The transition from quadratic to linear complexity has practical implications for hardware performance. Standard attention implementations, even highly optimized ones like FlashAttention, eventually succumb to the quadratic cost as the sequence length $n$ grows.

PoM's computational cost is primarily determined by the hidden dimension $D$ and the polynomial degree $k$. The analysis shows that PoM starts to outperform MHA when the sequence length exceeds a relatively small threshold (often around 1,000 tokens for common model sizes). 

Empirical tests demonstrate this efficiency clearly:
- **Throughput:** In language modeling tasks with a sequence length of 32,000 tokens, PoM-based models achieved a throughput of approximately 447,500 tokens per second, while standard MHA models dropped to roughly 52,400 tokens per second.
- **Scaling:** Unlike attention, where doubling the resolution of an image leads to a fourfold increase in token-mixing time, PoM's scaling is direct. In image generation tasks at 1024x1024 resolution, PoM-based architectures were twice as fast as their attention-based counterparts.

Importantly, these speedups were achieved using high-level PyTorch code. The authors suggest that further optimizations using custom CUDA kernels could increase this advantage even further.

## Performance Across Modalities: From Text to 3D Points

To establish PoM as a general-purpose building block, it was evaluated across five distinct domains.

### Natural Language Processing
In language modeling, the researchers replaced the attention layers in a 125M parameter GPT-2 model with PoM. While a pure PoM model performed slightly worse than standard attention, a "Hybrid" version—which combined PoM with some local attention (attention restricted to a small window of nearby tokens)—matched the performance of the original GPT-2 on benchmarks like ARC and MMLU while remaining significantly faster at long context lengths.

### Image Generation
For class-conditional image generation (ImageNet 256x256), PoM was integrated into "Scalable Interpolant Transformers" (SiT). The PoM-based models produced images of comparable visual quality to the original attention-based models, as measured by Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), but with twice the inference speed.

### Optical Character Recognition (OCR)
On the task of reading handwritten text, PoM-based models achieved Character Error Rates (CER) comparable to state-of-the-art attention models. Notably, as the text grew from single lines to multi-line documents (increasing the sequence length), the efficiency gains of PoM became more pronounced.

### Earth Observation and 3D Vision
In 3D point cloud segmentation (ScanNet) and satellite image time-series analysis (PASTIS), PoM models matched the accuracy of standard Transformers. In satellite analysis, where temporal sequences can be quite long, the PoM-based version was over 300% faster than the attention-based baseline. In 3D vision, PoM allowed for processing entire large-scale point clouds in a single pass without the need to "chunk" the data into smaller pieces, which is a common requirement for $O(n^2)$ models.

## Conclusion

The Polynomial Mixer (PoM) offers a scalable alternative to self-attention that bypasses the quadratic complexity bottleneck. By summarizing sequence information into a shared polynomial state, it achieves linear scaling without sacrificing the theoretical expressivity or the practical performance of the Transformer architecture. Its versatility across text, images, 3D points, and satellite data suggests that it can serve as a robust, general-purpose component in the next generation of efficient, long-context AI models.

keyInsights: PoM is theoretically proven to be permutation equivariant and, when combined with positional encoding, forms a universal sequence-to-sequence approximator.
PoM satisfies the 'contextual mapping property,' allowing each token to derive a unique value from the entire sequence while retaining its identity, crucial for Transformer expressivity.

The linear computational complexity of PoM ($O(n)$) offers substantial efficiency and scalability advantages over MHA's quadratic complexity ($O(n^2)$) for processing long sequences without sacrificing performance.
originalProblem: Multi-Head Attention (MHA) in Transformers incurs quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to the input sequence length.
This quadratic scaling limits the application of Transformers to tasks involving very long sequences, such as high-resolution images, videos, or extensive text.
Existing linear-time alternatives often compromise performance, impose fixed sequence lengths, or are limited by causal ordering, restricting their general applicability.
results: PoM-based models achieved performance on par with or slightly better than MHA across diverse tasks including NLP, OCR, Earth observation, 3D point cloud segmentation, and image generation.
Models incorporating PoM demonstrated significant speedups, such as 2x faster throughput for image generation and over 300% faster inference for Earth observation compared to MHA baselines.

PoM's linear scaling enabled efficient processing of entire point clouds without chunking and provided substantial throughput advantages over highly optimized attention (e.g., FlashAttention) for long sequence lengths.
solution: PoM aggregates input tokens into a compact, shared representation using a learned polynomial function, allowing each token to query this context in a linear-time manner.
The PolyMorpher block integrates PoM as a drop-in replacement for MHA, preserving the Transformer's residual structure, and can be adapted for causal sequences with O(1) autoregressive inference.
The architecture ensures all internal operations scale linearly with sequence length, addressing the efficiency bottleneck of MHA.
summary.summary: The Polynomial Mixer (PoM) introduces a linear-time token mixing mechanism as a direct replacement for the quadratically scaling Multi-Head Attention in Transformer architectures. PoM-based models achieve comparable or superior performance across five diverse domains, demonstrating up to 2x faster throughput and significant scalability improvements for long sequence lengths.

title: PoM: A Linear-Time Replacement for Attention with the Polynomial Mixer
